# `liber:security@1`

Generated reference for the `liber:security` package (`security.lsidl`). See [the LSIDL language](../../../LSIDL.md).

LiberSystem security package - permissions and capability management.

## Imports

- `error` from [`liber:base@1`](/docs/gen/liber/base/v1.md)
- `start-result as process-start-result` from [`liber:process@1`](/docs/gen/liber/process/v1.md)

## Types

### enum `capability`

The kinds of capability a component may be declared to receive: a connection to one of the system services. The typed vocabulary a permission manifest is written in - a grant names a service the component may reach, never an ambient right. A component holds exactly the capabilities its manifest lists and can reach nothing else.

| case | ordinal | description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `log` | 0 |  |
| `storage` | 1 |  |
| `network` | 2 |  |
| `device` | 3 |  |
| `config` | 4 |  |
| `time` | 5 |  |
| `audio` | 6 |  |
| `input` | 7 |  |
| `graph` | 8 |  |
| `resource` | 9 |  |
| `process` | 10 |  |
| `permission` | 11 |  |
| `supervisor` | 12 |  |
| `volumes` | 13 |  |
| `services` | 14 |  |
| `usb` | 15 |  |
| `display` | 16 |  |
| `input-keys` | 17 |  |
| `audio-stream` | 18 |  |

### record `manifest`

A component's permission manifest: its name, the capabilities it requests (the set a packaged component will ship on disk), and the exact set it is granted - the audited intersection of the request with the manager's policy. This typed object is the source of truth for what a component may be granted - never a parsed text / JSON file. A launched component starts with the granted capabilities and nothing else (the sandbox); a requested capability the policy withholds surfaces as an audited denial, and the textual form a tool might render is only ever a view of this object.

| field | type | description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `component` | `string` |  |
| `requested` | `list<capability>` |  |
| `grants` | `list<capability>` |  |

### record `audit-entry`

One decision in the PermissionManager audit trail: the component a grant decision was made for, the capability in question, whether it was granted, and whether the decision was a runtime permission request rather than a static manifest grant. A `dynamic` entry records a capability the component asked for at launch that its manifest did not declare - decided by the headless policy default and recorded back into the same store - while a static (`dynamic` false) entry is a decision over the component's declared manifest. The policy layer's record of every authority decision, queryable over the typed API the same way the journal is.

| field | type | description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `component` | `string` |  |
| `capability` | `capability` |  |
| `granted` | `bool` |  |
| `dynamic` | `bool` |  |

## Interfaces

### interface `permission`

The PermissionManager contract: the policy over the kernel's capability mechanism. `lookup` returns a component's declared permission manifest (the typed source of truth); `audit` returns the trail of every grant / deny decision the manager has made; `run` is the launcher / granter primitive - it starts a named system tool (a shell command run as its own ELF) under its manifest, granting it exactly its declared capabilities and nothing else, forwards the caller's stdout console, argument string, and inherited working directory to it, and returns the live process handle for job control. The granted clients are transferred to the new process out-of-band over the bootstrap channel the manager drives ProcessService to create; the manager is the launcher / granter, ProcessService the mechanism. A caller (the shell) reaches the OS tools only through this op, never the raw kernel loader.

Request `[op u16][corr u32][args]`, reply `[corr u32][result]`.

| op | method | signature | description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | `lookup` | `lookup(component: string) -> result<manifest, error>` |  |
| 2 | `audit` | `audit() -> stream<audit-entry>` |  |
| 3 | `run` | `run(name: string, args: string, cwd: string, stdout: handle<channel>) -> result<process-start-result, error>` |  |
